original_abstract: This study characterized the physicochemical and antioxidant properties of ten honey samples produced by *Apis mellifera* L. in the state of Roraima, Brazil. We specifically evaluated total phenolic content, flavonoid profiles, color intensity, and radical scavenging activity. Total phenolic levels, determined via the Folin-Ciocalteu method, ranged from 250 to 548 mg gallic acid equivalents per kilogram of honey. Flavonoid concentrations were assessed using two distinct approaches: the aluminum chloride method for total flavones/flavonols (yielding 9 to 48.6 mg quercetin kg⁻¹) and the 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine method for flavanones/dihydroflavonols (yielding 1805 to 2606 mg pinocembrin kg⁻¹). Antioxidant capacity was measured using the DPPH radical scavenging assay, with inhibitory concentrations (IC₅₀) ranging from 3.17 to 8.79 mg/mL. Statistical analysis revealed significant positive correlations between color intensity, flavone/flavonol levels, and total phenolic content, while these parameters were negatively correlated with the IC₅₀ values, indicating that darker honeys with higher polyphenolic content possess stronger antioxidant potential.
humanized_abstract: Here we investigated physicochemical and antioxidant features of 10 honeys produced by *Apis mellifera* L. from apiaries in Roraima state, Brazil. Total phenolic content, flavonoid composition and honey color intensity and the ability of honey extracts to scavenge free radicals were determined. Total phenolic content assessed using Folin-Ciocalteu method varied from 250 to 548 mg gallic acid equivalent per kilogram of honey. Flavonoids were quantified as total flavones/flavonols using aluminium chloride method (9–48.6 mg quercetin per kilogram of honey) and flavanones/dihydroflavonols using 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine method (1805–2606 mg pinocembran per kilogram of honey). The ability of honey extracts to scavengege the free radicals assessed by using the DPPH assay showed that inhibitory concentrations at 50 per cent (IC₅₀) values for honey extracts varied from 3.17 to 8.79 mg/mL. High degree of correlations between honey color intensity, total flavones/flavonols content, total phenolic content and DPPH IC₅₀ values were confirmed by factor analysis indicating that stronger antioxidant activities in darker honey samples with higher polyphenolic content.

The ability of honey extracts to scavengege the free radicals assessed by using the DPPH assay showed that inhibitory concentrations at 50 per cent (IC₅₀) values for honey extracts varied from 3.17 to 8.79 mg/mL.
